intro: Achillea millefolium, commonly known as yarrow, has been used for centuries in traditional medicine to produce a natural gum. This gum is derived from the plant's resin, which is collected from the stems and leaves, and is said to have antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties. Yarrow gum has been traditionally used to soothe mouth sores, gum inflammation, and other oral health issues.
side_effects: Achillea millefolium gum can have side effects such as digestive issues, allergic reactions, and interactions with certain medications, including blood thinners and diabetes medications, due to its active compounds like sesquiterpene lactones and flavonoids. Some users may experience nausea, dizziness, or stomach upset after consuming achillea millefolium gum. Additionally, high doses may exacerbate existing conditions, such as kidney or liver issues.

fever: Achillea millefolium gum, also known as yarrow gum, has been traditionally used to help alleviate fever, although its effectiveness in modern medicine is not extensively documented. recipe: To make achillea millefolium gum for medicinal use, gather the roots of the plant, dry them thoroughly, and then boil them in water to create a concentrated solution. This solution can be mixed with a gelling agent, such as gum arabic, to create a sticky gum.
